narrative: Dear [Student's Name], imagine the admissions team looking at your application like piecing together a story about your journey to the bar exam, and unfortunately, the chapters they've read so far paint a picture of real hurdles ahead. Your Year 3 grades placed you in the bottom decile at your school, which stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward a risk of not passing—it's like the model saw that late-stage struggle as the biggest red flag for what's ahead. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile, also at the bottom, suggesting those early challenges set a pattern that's hard to shake. Your LSAT score of 29.5 added to that concern, as it fell short of what the model associates with bar success in similar profiles. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.7, it wasn't enough to counterbalance those trends, ranking as a moderate pull toward failure. Your upper-middle family income, Asian background, male gender, and full-time student status all layered on subtle risks too, based on patterns the model has seen in past students who faced similar combinations. At 40% predicted chance of bar failure, it's not a sure thing by any means, but enough to make them pause on your law school spot. This isn't about doubting your potential—your UGPA shows real academic strength—but about how these pieces together whisper caution. You've got time to rewrite this story: targeting a higher LSAT retake or bolstering those later grades could shift the narrative dramatically. We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong and pass that bar with flying colors. What steps will you take next to strengthen your profile?
